name: Revati
shortDescription: The only daughter of King Kakudmi, Revati's story involves traveling to Brahma's realm and marrying Balarama, an avatar of Shesha.
description: Revati is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, primarily known as the daughter of King Kakudmi and the wife of Balarama, the elder brother of Krishna. Her story is most elaborately narrated in the Bhagavata Purana. King Kakudmi, feeling that no man was worthy of his beautiful and accomplished daughter, took Revati to Brahmaloka, the abode of Brahma, seeking his counsel. Upon their arrival, they discovered that time flows differently in the celestial realms, and they had to wait a considerable period. When they finally met Brahma, he laughed and explained that many ages had passed on Earth, and all potential suitors Revati had in mind were long gone. Brahma advised Kakudmi to return to Earth and marry Revati to Balarama, an avatar of Shesha, the serpent on which Vishnu rests. This union was destined to restore balance to the Earth, which was burdened by the weight of its inhabitants. Revati's story highlights the vastness of cosmic time scales and the importance of divine intervention in human affairs.
subtitle: The princess who traveled through time.
role: Wife of Balarama; Symbol of beauty and destiny

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Revati's origin is rooted in the lineage of King Kakudmi, a powerful and righteous ruler. Her beauty and virtues were unparalleled, leading her father to seek a worthy husband for her. The primary myth surrounding Revati involves her journey with King Kakudmi to Brahmaloka. This journey is significant because it highlights the concept of time dilation in Hindu cosmology. Upon returning to Earth, Kakudmi and Revati found that the world had changed drastically. The people were smaller and less virtuous. Following Brahma's advice, Kakudmi arranged for Revati to marry Balarama. Balarama, using his divine strength, shortened Revati's stature to match the average height of people in that era, demonstrating his power and ensuring a harmonious union.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Revati's role is primarily defined by her marriage to Balarama, a significant figure in the Vaishnava tradition. As Balarama's wife, she plays a supportive role in his divine mission to alleviate the burden of the Earth. Her story is significant because it underscores the cyclical nature of time in Hindu cosmology and the importance of divine intervention in restoring balance to the world. She represents the enduring power of destiny and the ability to adapt to changing circumstances. Her tale also serves as a reminder of the vastness of the cosmos and the relative insignificance of human timescales in the face of divine time.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Revati symbolizes beauty, grace, and the acceptance of destiny. Her journey through time symbolizes the transient nature of human existence and the importance of aligning oneself with the divine will. The act of Balarama shortening her height can be interpreted as a metaphor for adapting to the needs of a new era and finding harmony in change. Her story also symbolizes the importance of seeking guidance from higher powers when faced with difficult decisions, as exemplified by King Kakudmi's consultation with Brahma. Revati's character embodies the ideal of a devoted wife and a woman who embraces her fate with courage and dignity.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Revati's legacy is primarily preserved through the Puranic literature, particularly the Bhagavata Purana. While she doesn't have widespread independent worship, her story is often recounted during religious discourses and celebrations related to Krishna and Balarama. Her tale continues to inspire discussions about time, destiny, and the role of divine intervention in human affairs. In modern interpretations, Revati's story can be seen as a reminder of the importance of adaptability and resilience in the face of change. Her character also serves as a symbol of feminine virtue and devotion.

sources.author: A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada
sources.description: English translation and commentary on the Bhagavata Purana, a primary source for Revati's story.

sources.author: K.M. Munshi
sources.description: A retelling of the Krishna story, including the narrative of Balarama and Revati.

associations.name: Time
associations.significance: Her story revolves around the concept of time dilation and the differences between earthly and celestial time.

associations.name: Destiny
associations.significance: Revati's marriage to Balarama was preordained, highlighting the importance of destiny in her life.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Rip Van Winkle
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Revati and Rip Van Winkle experience a significant passage of time while remaining unchanged themselves, leading to their return to a drastically different world.

companions.name: Balarama
companions.description: Husband of Revati; an avatar of Shesha and the elder brother of Krishna.

companions.name: Kakudmi
companions.description: Father of Revati; a righteous king who sought a suitable husband for his daughter.

cultural.modernLegacy: Revati's story is sometimes used in modern discourse to illustrate the concept of time dilation and the challenges of adapting to change. She remains a significant, if less overtly celebrated, figure in Hindu mythology.
